front: Composer: Italian bel canto composer; frequent collaborator with Felice Romani; beloved by Wagner for his long melodic lines; died at age 33
Composer: his tomb is inscribed with the first line of the 'flower aria' from La sonnambula
Composer: Maria Callas's signature roles include Norma and Imogene (from Il pirata); Joan Sutherland ('La Stupenda') was another prime interpreter
Composer: I Capuleti e i Montecchi is based on Romeo and Juliet; first collaboration with Felice Romani was Il pirata
Composer: Beverly Sills also led the bel canto revival alongside Callas and Sutherland; Donizetti wrote 30 operas before finding success, while this composer succeeded young
Composer: Callas shocked the opera world in 1949 by singing Elvira in I puritani days after Brunnhilde in Die Walkure; Jenny Lind got her big break during a Berlin production of his Norma
Opera: Druid priestess Norma has a secret affair with Roman proconsul Pollione; both are burnt alive at the end
Opera: Adalgisa is Pollione's other love interest; Pollione urges her to come to Rome in 'Vieni in Roma'; Norma eventually entrusts Pollione's children to her
Opera: Pollione sings 'Meco all'altar di Venere' recalling a dream where Norma sends lightning to smite his beloved; Norma contemplates killing her children in 'Teneri, teneri figli'
Opera: the chorus 'Guerra, guerra!' incites the Celts to war against Rome; Norma and Pollione sing the duet 'In mia man alfin tu sei'
Opera: Giuditta Pasta premiered Norma and requested 'Casta diva' be transposed from G to F major; Norma and Adalgisa sing 'Si fino all'ore estreme' and 'Sola, furtiva, al tempio'
Opera: Rosa Ponselle caused a sensation in the Met's 1927 revival; Callas cancelled after Act 1 at 1958 Rome Opera House; opening chorus 'Dell'aura tua profetica'
Opera: Norma's Act 1 cavatina praying to the 'chaste goddess' (the moon) while raising mistletoe
Opera: F major, 12/8 time; begins with soprano sustaining a messa di voce A4 for seven-and-a-half beats; concludes with two-octave coloratura on 'ah, si'
Opera: Callas performed it 89 times in concert; originally in G major, transposed to F at Giuditta Pasta's request; Caballe's 1974 performance at the Roman Theatre of Orange
Opera: village girl Amina sleepwalks across a rickety mill bridge; the village phantom turns out to be her
Opera: 'Ah! non credea mirarti' -- Amina laments withered flowers while sleepwalking, with oboe interjections; first line inscribed on the composer's tomb
Opera: Elvino takes back his engagement ring, encouraged by innkeeper Lisa; Amina ends up in Count Rodolfo's room by accident
Opera: 2009 Met production controversially set in a modern New York rehearsal hall; audiences booed
Opera: 1955 La Scala production conducted by Leonard Bernstein, directed by Luchino Visconti, with Callas; credited with purging excessively flashy soprano antics
Opera: Bellini's final opera, set during the English Civil War; Elvira loves Royalist lord Arturo; ends with their marriage after Cromwell declares a pardon
Opera: tenor role Arturo sings an F5 in 'Credeasi, misera' -- one of the highest notes in the standard tenor repertoire
Opera: Bellini told librettist Carlo Pepoli: 'Carve into your head in adamantine letters... people must weep, shudder, die through singing'
Opera: Elvira's mad scene 'Qui la voce...Vien, diletto'; premiered by quartet Lablache, Rubini, Tamburini, Giulia Grisi; based on 'Roundheads and Cavaliers'
Opera: Hexameron is Liszt-led variations on its theme; Fitzcarraldo ends with a shipboard performance; 'Suoni la tromba' bass-baritone duet ends Act 2
Opera: Enrichetta escapes using a borrowed wedding veil; Rossini suggested adding an act; Luigi Bassi wrote a clarinet fantasy based on its themes
